items.title: Fact check: Poilievre and Carney on Canada’s economy
items.summary_auto: Canada’s economy has been the focus of question period in the House of Commons. CBC’s Fact Check team takes a look at recent claims made by both Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Prime Minister Mark Carney.

items.title: Cricket Canada president's Surrey home targeted in extortion-related shooting for 2nd time: police
items.summary_auto: Surrey police say officers are investigating a shooting Thursday morning at a home in the South Newton neighbourhood. The same home was previously shot at on May 20.

items.title: Maxime Crépeau named Canada's starting goalkeeper for World Cup
items.summary_auto: Maxime Crepeau will be Canada's starting goalkeeper at the FIFA World Cup. Canada coach Jesse Marsch announced the decision at a press conference Thursday, handing Crepeau the No. 1 job after a months-long battle with Dayne St. Clair.

items.title: Let's go girls! Shania Twain biopic in the works at Sony Pictures
items.summary_auto: It's true, Twainiacs. The country-pop icon's life story is reportedly headed for the big screen, with a biopic adaptation in the works for Sony Pictures co-produced by the five-time Grammy winner herself.

items.title: Finance minister stands his ground as budget watchdog predicts higher deficits
items.summary_auto: The federal finance minister is standing by his deficit projections after Ottawa's budgetary watchdog cast doubt on the government's ability to hit a key fiscal anchor.

items.title: Agricultural giant at centre of urban-rural housing divide in Ontario border city
items.summary_auto: Cargill is trying to block townhomes and an apartment complex from being built in Sarnia by using the province's Minister's Zoning Order for the opposite reason it was originally intended.

items.title: FIFA no longer letting fans bring refillable water bottles into World Cup stadiums
items.summary_auto: While ticket prices are sky-high and matches are scheduled in the heat of summer, FIFA says it decided to prohibit fans from bringing their own water bottles into stadiums “to prohibit bottles to prevent risk and injury to players and attendees.”

items.title: Seth Rogen, Evan Goldberg vow big adventures to come with Littlest Hobo revival
items.summary_auto: Canadian friends Seth Rogen and Evan Goldberg are leaning into nostalgia, reimagining Cancon classic The Littlest Hobo for new audiences. The project is set to be an hour-long, live-action scripted drama.

items.title: Hundreds protest Hamilton data centre proposal
items.summary_auto: Hamilton’s Committee of Adjustment is hearing an application from Slate Asset Management to split up an industrial lot. It is considering building a data centre on part of it. Hundreds of people came to Hamilton city hall on Thursday to oppose the application.

items.title: Strong display of the northern lights is possible across Canada tonight
items.summary_auto: Canadians could be in for another beautiful display of the northern lights tonight, as the sun unleashed several fast-moving particles in the Earth's direction over the past couple of days.

items.title: B.C. MLA Jordan Kealy charged with sexual assault in Fort St. John
items.summary_auto: The B.C. Prosecution Service says Independent MLA Jordan Kealy, who represents Peace River North, has been charged with one count of sexual assault in Fort St. John. Kealy says he is innocent and won't be resigning.

items.title: Shia LaBeouf sentenced after bar fight during Mardi Gras
items.summary_auto: Actor Shia LaBeouf was sentenced to probation Wednesday after pleading guilty to punching three people outside a New Orleans bar during Mardi Gras.

items.title: Man who killed his children granted conditional discharge: B.C. Review Board
items.summary_auto: The British Columbia Review Board has granted a conditional discharge for a man convicted in the brutal slayings of his three children.

items.title: Alberta court upholds sanctions against veterinarian with history of complaints
items.summary_auto: Alberta's highest court has upheld sanctions against an Edmonton veterinarian who unsafely sedated a cat and then actively avoided the grieving owners when they had been misled about the cremation of their pet’s remains.

items.title: No handshake and no photo as Russia's Andreeva beats Ukrainian Kostyuk to reach French Open final
items.summary_auto: Russian teenager Mirra Andreeva reached her first Grand Slam final by beating Marta Kostyuk of Ukraine 6-1, 6-3 at the French Open on Thursday.

items.title: French-Iranian director and author Marjane Satrapi dead at 56
items.summary_auto: Acclaimed Iranian-French cartoonist and filmmaker Marjane Satrapi, a prominent advocate for women's rights, has died at 56. French President Emmanuel Macron is among those paying tribute to the artist.

items.title: Happy rebirthday? Why these Canadians chose a new date to celebrate themselves
items.summary_auto: Some people choose to mark their birthday on a different date as a way to reflect identity, recovery and personal meaning.

items.title: Missing Mount Everest guide Dawa Sherpa found alive after crawling back to base camp
items.summary_auto: A Sherpa guide was found crawling to base camp on Mount Everest a week after he went missing and was reunited with his family after they'd given up hope he would return.

items.title: Hezbollah rejects Lebanon ceasefire brokered by U.S., Israeli military says it will continue attacks
items.summary_auto: The Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah rejected a new ceasefire in Lebanon on Thursday and Israel said it would not withdraw troops from the country, undermining U.S. President Donald Trump's efforts to halt fighting there and forge peace with Tehran.

items.title: 66 opioid calls in 1 day. This is how bad the overdose crisis is, Winnipeg first responders say
items.summary_auto: Front-line workers say Winnipeg is in the midst of a drug epidemic, driven by a surge in opioid overdoses that is killing users and overwhelming first responders who are dispatched to dozens of calls daily.

items.title: She thought it was a scam call, until she found out police identified her long-lost uncle
items.summary_auto: TPS Project 31 helped identify the remains of Willard Duval, an Indigenous man in his 70s who was reported missing in 2018, and connect him to his niece.

items.title: Oneida woman discovers long-lost uncle after Toronto police DNA testing identifies his remains
items.summary_auto: TPS Project 31 helped identify the remains of Willard Duval, an Indigenous man in his 70s who was reported missing in 2018, and connect him to his niece.

items.title: ‘I'm scared to go to bed at night’: Halifax residents raise concerns about rampant pest problems
items.summary_auto: Lindsay Eagleson was thrilled to find a newly renovated apartment in a Halifax neighbourhood after living in her car for 10 days. But before she moved her belongings into the building a couple months ago, she realized there was a pest infestation problem.

items.title: Ontario will approach civil service return-to-office during FIFA with 'flexibility,' minister says
items.summary_auto: Ontario’s finance minister says there will be "flexibility" to rules requiring all civil servants to work five days a week in the office during the FIFA World Cup, as unions representing those workers say disruptions associated with the games highlight how rigid and confusing the policy has become.

items.title: Quebec girl’s death highlights lack of regulations for bouncy castles
items.summary_auto: The tragic death of a three-year-old girl after a bouncy castle was swept up by the wind in Montreal is raising questions about the safety of the devices and the lack of regulation.

items.title: Her husband is accused in an AI deepfake investigation. She’s filing for divorce
items.summary_auto: The wife of a man accused in an AI deepfake investigation is speaking out to express support for the alleged victims, to call for stronger laws to deal with the kind of behaviour of which her husband stands accused, and to start a conversation about support available for people in her position, who are not formally victims of alleged crimes, but remain deeply affected.

items.title: Supersized data centres are coming to Canada. One province is at the epicentre
items.summary_auto: A new national AI strategy by the federal government this week comes at a time when the country is confronting a wave of new high-powered data centres, while public sentiment could be souring on the impacts of the new technology.

items.title: 1 year after acquittal in Hockey Canada sexual assault trial, Carter Hart could win Stanley Cup
items.summary_auto: As the Vegas Golden Knights advance in the Stanley Cup Final, at the centre of their championship run is Canadian goaltender Carter Hart — whose journey has been highly successful on ice and deeply controversial off it.

items.title: Higher oil and gas prices coming soon, industry and analysts warn
items.summary_auto: Energy executives and analysts are warning that oil and gas prices will likely rise significantly as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed and stubborn demand burns through strategic reserves.

items.title: Jet fuel shortage? Don't worry about it, say major airlines
items.summary_auto: More than six weeks after international warnings that Europe could run out of jet fuel, at least two major global carriers are saying it's safe to book a summer holiday to Europe because they don't expect the expensive commodity to run out during the busy travel season.

items.title: YouTube already had a place in Hollywood. The success of Backrooms and Obsession prove it's undeniable
items.summary_auto: Summer movie season is normally a battle of Hollywood heavyweights — but this year, two of the hottest tickets at the box office come from young YouTubers who brought their built-in audiences with them to the big screen. Industry watchers say their success could influence what projects get made in the future.

items.title: Canada's forestry crisis should be blamed on homegrown problems, not Trump, federal report says
items.summary_auto: The report says obstacles include unstable access to affordable fibre, excessive regulations, persistent underinvestment in manufacturing, weak capacity to innovate, and inadequate domestic demand for wood-based products.

items.title: Emaciated grey whales washing up on B.C. beaches 'canary in coal mine,' researcher warns
items.summary_auto: Two more grey whales have washed up in B.C., with researchers and experts both warning the province is on pace for a record breaking year.

items.title: Tornado warnings again pop up around southern Manitoba
items.summary_auto: People in southern Manitoba were subjected to another evening of tornado warnings on Wednesday, one day after three twisters touched down in the region.
